CANAL, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Alentejo, comarca and 23 m. NE of Evora.
CANAL, a township of Venango co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U. S., 218 m. NW of Harrisburg. Pop. in 1840, 867.
CANAL (EL), a town of Spain, in New Castile, prov. and 4 m. N of Guadalaxara, on the r. bank of the Henares.

CANALE, a town of Piedmont, cap. of a mandemento, in the prov. and 7 m. NW of Alba, and 25 m. SE of Turin, on an affluent of the Bourbo, at an alt. of 708 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 3,500. In the environs are springs of neutral salts.-Also a town of Illyria, in the gov. of Trieste, circle and 9 m. N of Goritz, on the l. bank of the Isonzo, 30 m. above the entrance of that river into the Adriatic, and at an alt. of 380 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 1,000.
CANALEJAS, a town of Spain, in Leon, prov. and 40 m. ENE of the town of that name.—Also a town of Old Castile, 27 m. ESE of Valladolid.

CANALES, a town of Spain, in Leon, prov. and 17 m. NW of the town of that name, near the r. bank of the Orvigo.—Also a town in the prov. and 19 m. ESE of Valladolid.—Also a town of Old Castile, in the prov. of Soria, 41 m. SSW of Logrono, at the foot of the Sierra de San Lorenzo.—Also a town in the prov. and 13 m. WSW of Avila.
CANAL FULTON, a village of Lawrence township, in Tuscarawas co., state of Ohio, U. S., on the E of Tuscarawas river, and on the Ohio canal. Pop. in 1840, about 400.
CANALIA, a town of Turkey in Europe, in Trikalia or Thessaly, on the SE bank of Lake Carlas, 5 m. NNE of Velestrino, and 10 m. NW of Volo.
CANAMARES, a town of Spain, in New Castile, prov. and 25 m. NNW of Cuenca, on the l. bank of the Escabas.
CANAMERO, a town of Spain, in Estremadura, prov. and 87 m. E of Badajoz, and 29 m. ENE of Villanueva de la Serena, at the foot of the Sierra de la Guadaloupe.

CANANDAIGUA, a township, cap. of Ontario co., in the state of New York, U.S. It presents a finely diversified, and generally fertile and well-cultivated surface. Pop. in 1840, 5,602. The village is delightfully situated at the N extremity of a lake of the same name, and is neatly and well-built. Pop. 2,790.—C. lake is 14 m. in length, and from 1 to 11 m. in breadth. Its waters are clear, and abound with fish, and its shores present rich cultivation. It discharges itself by Flint creek into Seneca river.

CANANEA, a small maritime town of Brazil, in the prov. of Sao Paulo, on an island in the bay of the same name, 145 m. SW of Sao Paulo, and in 25° 3' S lat. Pop. 3,000. The channel by which the island is separated from the continent is navigable by large vessels, but the port can be entered by those only of smaller dimensions. It possesses, however, extensive building-docks. The district belonging to the municipality is well-watered, and produces large quantities of rice, and in the higher tracts coffee and vanilla.—The bay of C., formerly called Tarapande, forms an irregularly shaped embrasure, 10 m. in depth, and about half that extent in breadth; enclosed on the S by the lofty peninsula of the same name, and on the N by the low sandy islands of Iguape and Cananea. Its entrance is considerably obstructed by sand-banks. CANANORE, KANURA, or KANNANU'R, a town and principality of Hindostan, at the bottom of a small bay on the coast of Malabar. It was early possessed by the Portuguese, who, about the year 1605, obtained leave of the king of the country to build a fort, which they secured with a strong garrison. Having thus become a point of communication with Europe, and being well-supplied from the adjoining country with rice, pepper, sugar, cardamoms, ginger, tamarinds, and other valuable commodities, it soon CAN rose to be a populous and commercial city; and, about the middle of the 18th cent., when it was under the power of the Dutch, who had established a considerable factory at this place, nearly 200 vessels arrived annually in its harbour. In 1770, C. was sold by the Dutch, to the ancestors of the present reigning family, for 100,000 rupees. It was afterwards seized by Tippu Saib; but is now subject to the British, who, under General Abercrombie, took possession of the fort on the 17th of Dec., 1790. The biby, or lady of Cananore, however, who managed the affairs of the royal family during the minority of her son, was allowed to retain the nominal sovereignty of the district, and to collect all the revenues except the customs, but paid an annual land-tax of 14,000 rupees or £1,400 to the company. Her territories on the continent were very small, extending nowhere above 2 m. from the town. The surface is, in general, high and uneven; but it is all capable of cultivation, though a small part of it only is fitted for rice-ground. The biby possessed also most of the Laccadive islands, which, however, are so wretchedly poor that the tribute which she derived from them was altogether trifling. Her principal resources arose from trade which she carried on to a considerable extent in vessels of her own, with Arabia, Bengal, and Sumatra; and "her commercial affairs are so well managed," says Dr. Buchanan, "that she will soon, it is said, recover the losses she is alleged to have suffered from the rapacity of some British officers during the wars of Malabar."—The town of C., which is 45 m. NW of Calicut, in N lat. 11° 42', stretches about half-a-mile along the shore, and is defended by a strong fortress, formerly considered impregnable, and several detached forts on every side. It is very narrow, except near the centre, where it runs a little way up into the land, and is terminated by a battery, called Spice fort. It contains several very good houses, which are possessed by Mahommedan merchants; but its E extremity is chiefly inhabited by fishermen, and consists only of a group of miserable huts. The fortress stands a little SW of the town, upon a promontory which projects a quarter of a mile into the sea. It has the complete command of the bay; and, since the province was ceded to the company, has been considerably strengthened with works after the European fashion. The sea surrounds it on all sides, except on the NW, where it is separated from the land by a deep ditch and strong fortifications. It contains the wharf, where vessels may lie with great safety during summer, but in winter it affords very little security; also an hospital, the chief's house, the warehouses, and lodgings for the different officers of the company. C. still possesses a flourishing trade, though its exports have been considerably diminished; and it employs from 25 to 30 vessels.—Buchanan's Journey through Mysore, &c., vol. ii.—Bartolomeo's Voyage, p. 144.— Oriental Repository, vol. i.

CANAR, or Alancanar, a town of New Grenada, 22 m. NNE of Cuenca. It contains the ruins of a palace of the Incas. The surrounding territory is well-watered and fertile, and possesses extensive mines of gold, silver, copper, &c.
CANARA, a town of the States-of-the-Church, in the delegation and 15 m. SE of Perugia, on the l. bank of the Topino, 10 m. ESE of the entrance of that river into the Tiber.

CANARA, a province of Hindostan, extending along the Malabar coast between the 12th and 15th degree of N lat., and 74° and 76° E long. Its length along the coast is 180 m., and it is from 30 to 90 m. in breadth. It is separated from Mysore, on the E, by the Western Ghauts; on the S it has the prov. of Malabar; on the W, the Indian ocean; and on the N, Bejapur; and comprehends the countries of Tulava and Haiga, with a small portion of Malayala on the S, and of Kankana on the N. Its name is supposed to be an European corruption of Karnata, a people residing above the Ghauts; and to have been bestowed upon it because it belonged to the princes of that nation; and we may observe, that, on the other side of the peninsula, the Carnatic received its name from the same source, when first conquered by the Moslems. It has an area of 7,380, or, according to another admeasurement, of 7,477 sq. m.; and is now divided into two districts,—North and South C. The surface is rugged and uneven; but is not intersected by any large river. Though the air is in general pure and pleasant, and the climate salubrious, yet in some places it is extremely unhealthy, particularly in the N part of the province. In Tulava, heavy rains and strong W winds prevail between the middle of May and the middle of August. The rain during the other seasons of the year comes from the E, and commonly falls in gentle showers. In the wintermonths, from November to March, the weather is dry, but the air is reckoned cold by the natives. The soil throughout the prov. is in general good; and produces abundance of rice, of which great quantities are exported to Europe and various parts of India: the best in quality is grown in the neighbourhood of the coast. In the neighbourhood of the mountains the rains are sometimes so excessive as greatly to injure the crops, but the inland part of the country is very favourable to plantations. Some of the mountains are covered with stately forests of various kinds of wood, among which the teak is the most valuable; indeed, the Western Ghauts in general present a very different appearance from those in the E. Instead of the naked sun-burnt peaks of the latter, the hills here, though steep and stony, are by no means rugged, and are covered with a rich mould. In Tulava, all the lands are private property; but in Haiga, the hills and forests, and in Sunda, a district of Kankana above the Ghauts, the arable lands belong to government. Every man pays a certain landtax, and cultivates his property in whatever manner he pleases. Some let their lands upon a lease of from 4 to 10 years; and the rent demanded is generally two morays of rice for every moray of land of the first quality; one and a half for middling land; and one for the worst land. The moray or mudi, a dry measure of Canara, is equal to 12525 bushel; and the moray or land measure, is equal to 49,005 square feet, or nearly 123 acre. The more wealthy cultivators keep from 20 to 25 ploughs; those in moderate circumstances have from 4 to 6; but the great proportion of farmers have only one. Out of the rent paid by the tenants, the proprietors contribute 60 per cent. to the government. Most of the cultivated lands are sown with rice. Of this plant they have a great variety of kinds, each of which requires a particular soil, and a particular method of culture. Some species produce, on land of good quality, three crops in the year; others only one; and they differ greatly from each other both in the quantity of the produce and in the quality of the grain. As much of the rice-ground is equally adapted for the rearing of sugar-canes, the cultivation of this article might be increased to a considerable extent; but the farmers consider rice a more profitable crop. Cocoa nuts, betel nuts, mangoes, pepper, cardamoms, turmeric, ginger, &c., are produced in great abundance; and several cucurbitaceous plants, besides a variety of kitchen-stuffs, are cultivated in every garden. Black pepper grows spontaneously in the woods, and wild nutmeg and cinnamon are very common. The forests abound with sandal, teak, and sissa trees, which furnish a considerable revenue to government. In the and Batticolah. S division of the province there were, in 1800, 247,218 morays of rice land in a state of cultivation, which employed 71,716 ploughs; and, besides forests, it contained 111,965; morays capable of culture, of which 24,181 morays were cleared for grass, 7,043 were capable of being converted into rice ground, and 1,789 were fit for gardens. According to a survey made in 1793, the garden-ground of this division contained 695,060 cocoa nut trees; 1,155,850 betel nuts; 59,772 mangoes; 368,828 pepper vines, and 54,362 o other descriptions. Since that time, however, it is supposed that the number of each kind has been fully doubled. In the N division, the proportion of lands under cultivation, and those capable of being so, are, to the sterile lands, nearly as 26.7 to 73.3. The number of ploughs in 1801 were 26,147; and the quantity of sugar annually produced was estimated at about 11,483 mounds of nearly 30 lbs. each. Buffaloes and oxen are almost the only cattle reared; and are chiefly bred in the districts near the Ghauts. A few swine are fed by some of the lower castes; but the inhabitants rear neither horses, sheep, goats, nor asses. Tigers are, in some districts, numerous, but there are no elephants. The number of horned cattle in the province is supposed to amount to 420,569, of which 97,356 are buffaloes. The commerce of C., since the country became subject to the company, and was thus freed from the ruinous exactions with which it was loaded by the sultans of Mysore, has assumed a more active appearance. Many wealthy merchants from Surat, Cutch, Bombay, and other places towards the N, have settled in the province. Rice is the grand article of exportation; which, together with betel-nut, cocoa-nut, pepper, sandal-wood, cinnamon, Terra Japonica, and turmeric, form a lucrative traffic with Surat, Bombay, Muscat, Cutch, Goa, Malabar, and the Mahrattah countries above the Ghauts. The principal imports are blue and white cotton cloths from Surat and Cutch; salt from Bombay and Goa; raw silk and sugar from China and Bengal; a kind of red dye called munjisht from Muscat; and oil and ghee from Surat. Great quantities of cloth are also brought from above the Ghauts by the Mahrattah merchants, and those of Bangalore and Cudappa. The revenue which the company derives from this country, arises chiefly from the duties upon commerce and the land-tax; and the produce of these, we may conclude, is very considerable, as the sea customs of the S division alone amounted in 1795 to 23,760 pagodas, or about £9,504 sterling. In 1836-7 it amounted to 2,758,460 rupees, of which 1,671,215 were derived from land. The inhabitants of C. are chiefly Hindus, and are divided into castes as in other parts of India, each of which has its peculiar manners and customs. The Canarese language is rather peculiar. It differs considerably from the Tamul, or what is called the Malabar language by the Europeans at Madras, though they are evidently branches of one dialect. The written characters are nearly the same, but in the Canarese there is a great admixture of words from different languages. In the southern division of Canara, there were in 1800, 79,856 houses, of which 2,545 were inhabited by Christians, 5,223 by Mahommedans, and the rest by Hindus and Pagans. The total pop. of the district was 396,672, of whom 7,924 were slaves. The houses in the N division, in 1800, amounted to 41,380, of which the Christians possessed 476, the Mahommedans 2,300, and Hindus and Pagans the remainder. The number of slaves was about 1,544. In 1807 the entire pop. was returned at 576,640.— The principal towns in C. are Mangalore, Barcelore, History.] C. before its subjugation by the sultans of Mysore, was possessed by several independent rajas. Many of these, upon its first conquest by Hyder Ali, were permitted to retain the management of the country, on paying an annual tribute, which they raised by a land-tax; and even when this permission was withdrawn, they still enjoyed some valuable privileges. Considerable allowances were granted to the temples and Brahmans: and the Christians-of whom great numbers of Kankana at noon: descent, had settled in Tulava-possessed, in quiet, many valuable estates in land. Upon the accession of Tippu, however, to the throne of his father, these allowances were reduced, and the estates of the Christians confiscated; their priests were thrown of the Hindus, and have always been able successfully to resist the pretensions of their governors to be proprietors of the soil. Upon the fall of Seringapatam, and the death of Tippu, when this country came into the possession of the British, they were even disposed to try how far they could assert their independence, and refused for a time to settle with the British collector of the revenue, until some decisive measures were adopted to punish the most obstinate and troublesome.—Buchanan's Journey from Madras through Mysore, Canara and Malabar, vol. iii.-Dellon Voyage aux Indes Orientales, p. 195.—Bartolomeo's Voyage to the East Indies, p. 103.—Madras Almanack.

CANARI, a commune of the island of Corsica, in the cant. of Nonza, on the E coast, to the N of Bastia. Pop. 994. It possesses a small port, the trade of which consists chiefly in fruit, legumes, and grain.
CANARI, a river of New Grenada, in the Llanos de Caguan, which, in union with several other streams, falls into the Japura, in 72° 18' W long.
CANARIAS, a small river of Brazil, in the prov. of Maranhao, which issues from the I. side of the Tutoya branch of the Parnahiba, and flows into the sea.

CANARIES, a group of islands in the N Atlantic ocean, supposed by some to be the same with the Insulæ Beatæ, or 'Fortunate isles,' of the ancients. They lie about 150 m. W from the coast of Africa, between 271 and 30° N lat., and the meridians of 131° and 181° W. The principal islands are seven in number: viz., Lancerota, Fuerta-Ventura, Grand Canary, Teneriffe, Gomera, Hiero or Ferro, and Palma; the smaller islands are Allegranza, Santa Clara, Graciosa, Roca, and Lobos. The united area of the seven principal islands is 2,360,676 fanegadas of 6,100 Spanish yards each, or 3,256 sq. m. Of this area about one-sixth part is under cultivation. Their geographical position and extent are as follow:- Surface Pop. Pop. Lat. in Long. in marine in sq. leagues. 1807. 1835. Geological structure.] From the appearance of the different strata which compose these islands, we may reasonably conclude that they are of volcanic origin. A great proportion of their surface is covered with calcined rock, pumice-stones, and black dust and ashes, which are evidently the remains of successive eruptions. The craters of several volcanoes are still discernible in many of the islands; and also the channels made by the lava which flowed from them; and so recently as 1824 a volcano burst out in Lancerota. Climate. The climate is delightful, and exceedingly salubrious. The rainy season continues from the beginning of December until March, which they call winter, though it seldom freezes, and snow is only to be seen on the tops of the mountains. It scarcely ever rains on the coast earlier than November. During summer, the sky is always serene, and a kind of black gum or pitch called bray. All the the heat is intense, particularly during the months of July, August, and September. A register of observations, kept at Santa Cruz in Teneriffe, from May 1808 to August 1810, gave the following mean temp. for each month in the year, as deduced from the daily mean of two observations made at sunrise and June December, Mean of the whole year 71°. It appears from the above that the temp. of the C. follows the law common to extra-tropical places: the greatest heat and the greatest cold are in the months following the solstices. The mean temp. of the coldest month, January, is the same as the mean temp. of the whole year in S Italy. The barometer during three years gave for the months of May, June, July, and August, 28 in. 3.173 lines; and from September to April, 28 in. 2.017 lines; the excess of summer over winter being 1.156 lines, or rather more than one-tenth of an inch. [Von Buch.] The climate, however, in these islands is far from being uniform; for while, in the towns upon the coast, the air is hot, dry, and calm, the inhabitants of the mountain-districts often experience fogs, wind, and even rain. From April to October, the NE tradewind prevails uninterruptedly: during the remaining months, the SW is the dominant wind. The centre of most of the islands rising into lofty mountains, which tower so far above the clouds as to intercept the current of the winds, a calm generally prevails on the lea-side of the island, or a gentle breeze from the opposite quarter. These calms or eddy winds, are extremely dangerous to small vessels approaching the islands; for, at the extremity of the smooth water, the waves break in all directions. The calms on the leeward of Grand Canary extend 20 or 25 leagues into the ocean; those of Teneriffe 15; of Gomera 10; and of Palma 30. Soil and productions.] Von Buch distinguishes five regions of vegetation in the C. islands, their great elevation affording so many successive zones of climate. The first is the zone of the cactus and euphorbia, extending to 1,200 ft. above sea-level; the 2d is that of European cultivation, between 1,200 and 2,500 ft.; the 3d is the zone of the woods, which extends from 2,500 to 4,880 ft.; the 4th is the The 5th embraces all above the latter elevation, and by the inhabitants tends little to its improvement. Lands are let, not for money, but for the half of the produce. The proprietor furnishes the seed, and other articles of the first necessity, and also the cattle, which indeed belong to him, and which he can take back at the expiry of the lease. For these he receives the half of the crop during the term of agreement, besides a fanega of wheat for every head of cattle. The peasants often rent the cattle without the land, on condition of sharing the half of their increase. Sugar was formerly made in great abundance; and twelve sugar-mills were employed in the manufacture of this article in Grand Canary alone. It has of late years, however, almost ceased to be made; and the cotton plantations have also perished. Besides sugar and cotton, olives, mulberries, and tobacco, might be cultivated with great success. The C. likewise produce barilla, orchilla, honey, wax, and islands furnish excellent wine; but the preference is in general given to those of Palma and Teneriffe. In good seasons, Teneriffe alone produces above 20,000 pipes, a third of which is Malmsey or Canary sack; the rest is a dry hard wine, which sells for about half the price of Malmsey. When two or three years old, it can scarcely be distinguished from Madeira; but when allowed to stand for four years, it turns mellow and sweet, and resembles the wine of Malaga in Spain. The following is a return of the chief productions of the C. islands in 1833: Wine. Barilla. Maize. Barley. Wheat. Qrs. Qrs. Quint. Qrs. Pipes. In addition to the above, 5,343 quarters of rye, 10,310 quarters of vegetables, 151,800 quarters of potatoes, and 1,498 quintals of orchilla were produced, and 1,275 lbs. of cochineal, which here feeds on the Cactus opuntia.—The cattle of the C. are, horses, oxen, goats, sheep, roebucks, and wild asses. They abound with several kinds of game, and poultry, and also with singing birds, particularly the Canary bird - Fringilla Canaria - so well known throughout Europe; and the shores swarm with fish, especially sturgeons and mackerel, which form a great source of nourishment to the poorer inhabitants. Commerce.] Teneriffe is the great centre of European commerce in these islands. Some ships go to Grand Canary and Palma, but their number is inconsiderable; and this branch of trade is chiefly in the hands of the English. The principal exports to Europe and the British colonies in America, are wines, brandy, fruits, orchilla-weed, and commodities which they receive from the Spanish West Indies. In 1846, the imports into Great Britain from the C. were 10 tons of barilla; 217 cwts. of cochineal; 147 packages of oranges and lemons; and 249,311 gallons of C. wine. From Great Britain they receive various kinds of woollen cloths, hats, silks, stockings, cotton-stuffs, hardware, spices, &c., also wheat when there is a scarcity in the islands, and beef, pork, butter, candles, tea, ale, and salt herring; from the N of Europe, linens, cordage, gunpowder, bar-iron, &c.; from the Mediterranean, cottons, silks, velvets, salt, oil, bass cordage, and innumerable small articles, either for home consumption, or for exportation to the Spanish West Indies; from North America, deal boards, pipe staves, bacallao or dried cod, hams, bees' wax, rice, &c. The commerce which these islands carry on with the Spanish settlements in the West Indies, is under particular regulations and restrictions. No foreign bottoms are permitted to be employed in this trade, nor are vessels allowed to be fitted out from any of the islands except Grand Canary, Teneriffe, and Palma. This trade is confined to their own produce, of which they annually export nearly 2,000 tons; but they also find opportunities of smuggling into the country immense quantities of European commodities. Besides this foreign commerce, the C. carry on a considerable trade with each other. The island of Teneriffe is the principal entrepot of all West India and European commodities, which it distributes among the other islands, and receives in exchange, from Grand Canary, provisions of all sorts, coarse woollen blankets, raw and wrought silk, flags, filtering stones, and some salt, &c.; from Palma, almonds, sugar, sweetmeats, boards, pitch, and raw silk; from Gomera and Ferro, raw silk, brandy, and cattle; from Lancerota and Fuerta-Ventura, corn, cattle, and fowls, also some salt and dried fish; and from all of them orchilla-weed. The port of Santa Cruz in Teneriffe was declared in 1838 to be a custom-house port of the first class; that is, open to commerce of every description, and to the produce, if not specially prohibited, of all countries. The ports of Orotava in Teneriffe, and Los Palmas in Canary, were also declared custom-house ports of the second class. The merchants of Cadiz are, it is said, about to establish a line of steamers between that port, the C., and the Cape Verde islands. The Royal mail-packet company's steamers to Brazil touch at Santa Cruz in Teneriffe, which is 260 m. steaming from Funchal in Maof the islands from the produce of their own sheep. The inhabitants of the C. employ a considerable number of vessels in the fishery on the coast of Barbary, which, if properly encouraged by the Spaniards, might be rendered a source of great convenience and profit to these islands; but the trade is clogged with foolish and unreasonable duties; and the price is impolitically regulated by the magistrates. This fishery extends along the coast about 600 m., from Cape Blanco on the S, to the S extremity of Mount Atlas on the N. The vessels employed in this trade are from 15 to 50 tons burthen; the smallest carrying 15 men, and the largest 30; and the general place of rendezvous is at Porto de Luz in the island of Grand Canary. They make from eight to ten voyages in the year. The bulk of their cargoes consists of samas or bream; but they also catch a large quantity of tassarte, cod, anhoua, carbino, and other kinds of fish. The fish called tassarte tastes like a fat mackerel, and when dried cannot be distinguished from dried salmon; and the cod that is caught on this coast is considered by some as preferable to that of Newfoundland. The situation of the C. islands, the salubrity of their climate, the fertility of the soil, and the quality of their productions, would all conspire to render them a most valuable possession, if they were inhabited by an active and industrious people, and placed under a government by whom agriculture and commerce were encouraged and promoted on sound principles. Government.] The C. are governed by Spanish laws. The governor resides at Santa Cruz in Teneriffe. The military establishment is returned at 24,000 men. The territorial revenue is said to amount to 14,391,735 francs, and to cover the expenses of administration.—The three easterly islands form one bishopric; and the four westerly, another. The bishops are suffragan of Seville. Inhabitants.] The present inhabitants of the C., who are generally known by the name of Islenos or 'the islanders,' are almost wholly of Spanish origin. They emigrate in great numbers to the coast of Caraccas, and to the Philippines; and are a lively and ingenious race, and, it is said, pronounce the Spanish language with a peculiar sweetness. The aboriginal inhabitants of these islands were called Guanches; many of them perished in resisting their Spanish conqueror Alonzo de Lugo, and by a plague which broke out in 1494. This fine nation was almost extinct at the commencement of the 17th cent.; and Malte Brun affirms, that at this time there does not exist throughout the archipelago one native of the pure race. Their language is supposed to have borne considerable analogy to that of the African Berbers. Various opinions have been entertained concerning their origin; but the most probable account is that they were originally Libyans, who, upon the conquest of Barbary by the Arabs, fled thither to avoid falling into the hands of their victorious enemies. Glas has attempted to prove this by a comparison of the language of South Barbary with that of the natives of the C. The languages of all the islands, except Teneriffe, though differing, had some affinity to each other, and were evidently branches of one original language; and their resemblance to the Shillha, and other dialects of the Libyan tongue, is a strong presumption that they derived their origin from the same stock. This probability is also considerably strengthened by the similitude of customs observed between the inhabitants of S Barbary and the C. Thus: the principal dish of the Libyans, called couscoussou, was much the same with the goffio of the C. They both poured hot butter into their wounds; and both fattened their daughters with milk before they gave them in marriage. The language of Teneriffe, however, was perfectly different from that of the other islands; and, according to Glas, it seems to have had some resemblance to the Peruvian, or some other of the American tongues. Since the conquest of these islands, the language and customs of their aboriginal inhabitants have been almost entirely lost in those of their conquerors. The natives all use the Castilian language; and, in their dress, manners, and appearance, resemble their fellow-subjects in the peninsula. Their complexions, however, are rather more swarthy; but they have good features, and fine large sparkling black eyes. The higher classes are in general poor, yet extremely polite and well-bred, and claim descent from some of the best families of Spain.—Histoire de la premiere decouverte et conquete des Canaries, par F. P. Bouthier, et Jean le Verrier. Paris, 1630, 8vo.—Glas's Description of the Canary Islands. -Viera's History of the Canaries. Bory de St. Vincent, Essai sur les Iles Fortunées.-Memoires de L'Institut National a Paris, tom. i.—Geographical Journal. -Humboldt.-Von Buch.
CANARINS, a tribe of Indians, in Brazil, descendants of the family of the Aimorés, who inhabit a village on the W side of the cordillera of the Aimorés, in the latitude of Villa Vicosa.

CANARY (GRAND), or CANARIA GRANDE, one of the Canary islands, which gives appellation to the group. It lies between the islands of Teneriffe and Fuerta- Ventura, and about 100 m. NW from Cape Bojador, on the African coast. The position of its NE point is N lat. 28° 13', W long. 15° 24\frac{1}{2}'; of its W point, N lat. 28° 1′ 20", W long. 15° 50½'. It received the epithet of Grand from John de Betancour, not on account of its size, but because of the strength, courage, and number of its inhabitants, who baffled all his attempts to subdue them. But the origin of the name Canary has never yet been determined. According to Pliny-if we may suppose his description to apply to this island—it was on account of its abounding with dogs of a very large size, two of which were presented to Juba, king of Mauritania. But if such animals ever did exist on this island, they were completely extinct when it was first visited by Europeans. Some modern authors derive it from a species of fruit abundant here, called by the Latins uva canina, or dog-grapes; others from an herb named canaria, which dogs eat in the spring to cause them to vomit; while others suppose—and with more apparent reason-that it received its name from its first inhabitants, who were probably a tribe of Africans, as Pliny mentions a people called Canarii, who dwelt beyond Mount Atlas, a part of Africa which lies adjacent to the C. islands. At a distance, this island has the appearance of a single mountain rising gradually from the circumf. to the centre. Its highest peak, El Cumbre, has an alt. of 6,648 ft. [Arlett.] It is about 12 leagues in length; 11 in breadth; and 40 in cir- cellent wheat, barley, maize, melons of different sorts, II. CAN cumf. Though high and mountainous, yet near the sea-coast there are numerous plains, and more level ground than in any of the neighbouring islands. At the NE end is a small peninsula about 2 leagues in circumf., which is joined to the main land by a narrow isthmus 2 m. long, and about a quarter broad. On the S side of this isthmus is a spacious bay, called Porto de Luz, which forms a capital roadstead for shipping of any burthen, and in which the water, at the landing-place, is generally so smooth that a boat may lie broadside to the shore without danger. About a league to the W of this bay is the city of Palmas, the capital, and the seat of the royal audiencia, or sovereign council of the Canaries. It is a place of no strength, but of considerable extent, and contains several fine buildings, particularly the cathedral of St. Anne. The houses, though only one story high, are in general elegantly built and flat at the top. It has 4 convents; and its population is estimated at about 18,000. The other ports are Gando and Gaete; and, excepting at these places, the whole coast is inaccessible to boats or vessels, on account of the high surf which is continually breaking upon it. Its inland towns or villages are Galdar, Telde, Tirachana, and Luz.—The pop. of the island in 1742 was estimated at 33,864; in 1835, at 68,040.—This island possesses one of the finest climates in the world. Nowhere, in the same latitude, are the heats of summer so temperate; and nowhere do the natives enjoy such health and longevity. The following table exhibits the result of a register kept at Las Palmas during 10 years, the time of observation being noon. May, . 70.5 December, . June, . . . . 646 The heat of this island, instead of diminishing in September and October, as at Santa Cruz, according to the table inserted in the preceding article, continues to augment until the middle of October. In consequence the palm-trees—from which Las Palmas takes its name—yield ripe dates freely on this island; whereas in Teneriffe they do not ripen fruit. The Euphorbia balsamifera is found here as high as 800 ft., forming bushes 10 or 12 ft. high, whereas at Santa Cruz it hardly rises above the ground. The tamarind tree here grows as large as the lime tree in Europe; and the Curica Papaya also flourishes exuberantly. [Von Buch.] The only disagreeable weather experienced is when the island is visited by a SE wind from the great desert of Africa. This wind is hot, dry, and stifling; and does much damage to the fruits of the earth by its pernicious quality; it is also generally attended with immense clouds of locusts, which devour every green herb upon which they alight. Happily, however, it blows very rarely, and never lasts long.—The soil of this island, though light and sandy, is covered with a rich mould, and yields two harvests in the year. It is well-watered, and suited for every species of agriculture. The pine, palm, wild olive, laurel, poplar, elder, bressos, drage tree, Terra nuepa or Lignum Rhodium, aloe, Indian fig, tubayba, euphorbium, tarrahala, and many others, grow spontaneously and without cultivation. Almonds, walnuts, chestnuts, apples, pears, peaches, apricots, cherries, plums, mulberries, figs, bananas, dates, oranges, lemons, citrons, limes, pomegranates, and all the American and European fruits, except the anana or pine-apple-which is not to be found in any of the Canaries-are abundant. The fertile districts of this island, however, bear no proportion to the stony, rocky, and barren ground, which covers almost sixsevenths of its surface; yet the inhabitants raise exworld, and many other kinds of vegetables; and the husbandman would be amply repaid for the labour of cultivation, were not the spirit of industry completely checked by the policy of the government, which prohibits the exportation of provisions in a plentiful season, and fixes a price upon them in the island; and by the system of strict entail which prevails. Many lands are, on this account, allowed to lie waste, which might, by a little labour and expense, be rendered abundantly fruitful, as they have abundant means of watering them in the numerous rivulets with which the island abounds. "The most fertile part of C.," says Glas, "is the mountain of Doramas, situated about 2 leagues from the city of Palmas. It is shaded by groves of different kinds of fragrant trees, whose lofty boughs are so thickly interwoven as to exclude the rays of the sun. The rills that water those shady groves, the whispering of the breeze among the trees, and the melody of the C. birds, form a most delightful concert. When a person is in one of these enchanting solitudes, he cannot fail of calling to remembrance the fine things the ancients have written of the Fortunate islands." The sugar plantations, which were formerly numerous and extensive in this island, and which employed 14 large manufactories, have now, in a great measure, given place to the cultivation of the vine. The wines and brandies of Grand C. have always been in great demand in the Spanish West Indies; and the inhabitants find it more profitable to exchange these for sugar than to raise it in their own country. The wine of this island, however, though good, has not such a body as that of Teneriffe, and is consequently not so fit for exportation.-The animals are camels, horses, asses, mules, bullocks, a few sheep, &c., also turkeys, geese, fowls, ducks, partridges, and C. birds.
CANAS, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, prov. and 26 m. SW of Lograno, and 6 m. SE of St. Domingo de la Calzada, on the l. bank of the Glara.
CANAS-DE-SENHORIM, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Beira, comarca and 11 m. S of Visen. It contains some saline sulphureous springs.
CANASERAGA CREEK, a river of the state of New York, U. S., in Madison co., which flows into the Chitteningo, 4 m. SSE of the entrance of that river into Oneida lake.
CANASGORDAS, a town of New Granada, in the prov. and 10 m. NW of Antioquia, near the source of the Sucio. Pop. 309.

CANASTOTA, a village of Lenox township, in Madison co., state of New York, U.S., 119 m. WNW of Albany, on the Erie canal, and near the Syracuse and Utica railway. It contained in 1840, 120 dwellings, and about 400 inhabitants.

CANASTRA (SERRA), an extensive ridge of Brazil, in the prov. of Minas-Geraes. It unites on the W with the Serra-da-Parida, and runs in an ESE direction along the N basin of the Rio Grande. The São-Francisco takes its rise in this chain.
CANATA, a town of Sicily, in the intendancy of Noto, district and 4 m. SSE of Modica. Horses and mules are extensively reared in the surrounding plain.
CANAVARALES, a town of New Granada, in the prov. of Magdalena, and 40 m. SW of Ocana, on the r. bank of the Canavarales, and 50 m. SE of the junction of that river with the Magdalena.
CANAVATE (EL), a town of Spain, in New Castile, prov. and 35 m. S of Cuenca, and 14 m. NNE of San Clemente.
CANAVERAL, a town in the prov. and 28 m. N of Caceres, and 12 m. SSE of Coria.

CANAVERAL (CAPE), a headland of the United States, on the E coast of Florida, in N lat. 28° 16½', CAN potatoes, yams, pompions, the best onions in the | W long. 80° 27'. It is surrounded by extensive and dangerous shoals.
CANAVERAL-DE-LEON, a town of Spain, in Estremadura, prov. and 60 m. SSE of Badajoz, and 33 m. SW of Llerena.
CANAVERAS, a town of Spain, in New Castile, prov. and 20 m. NNW of Cuenca.
CANA-VERDE, a small modern town of Brazil, in the prov. of Minas-Geraes, 8 m. from Tamandua. Pop. about 3,000. Agriculture and the rearing of cattle form the chief branches of local industry.
CANAVERUELAS, a town of Spain, in New Castile, prov. and 45 m. NW of Cuenca, and 20 m. N of Huete, near the l. bank of the Guadiela.
CANAVESE, or CANAVEZ, a district of Piedmont, on the Dora Baltea. Its cap. was Ivrea.

CANAVEZES, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Minho, comarca and 20 m. SSE of Guimaraens, and 30 m. SSE of Braga, on the l. bank of the Tamega. Pop. 1,500. In the environs are several sulphureous springs.
CANAVIERA, a bay of Brazil, to the N of the island of Santa-Catharina, in the district of N. S. das Necessidades.
CANAXUATO. See GUANAZUATO.

CANCALE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Ille-et-Vilaine, arrond. of St. Malo. The cant. comprises 6 com., and in 1831 contained a pop. of 14,503. The town is situated on the E side of the large bay of the same name, 9 m. E of St. Malo. Pop. 4,880. At the hamlet of La Houle is an excellent port. The adjacent rock of Cancale is celebrated for its oysters, the fishery of which forms one of the principal branches of local industry. In 1758 an unsuccessful descent was made by the English upon this part of the French coast.
CANCAN, a town of New Granada, in the prov. and 75 m. ENE of Antioquia, near the source of the S. Cruz, and 15 m. SSW of Remedios.

CANCANHULLY, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Mysore, on the r. bank of the Arkavutty, 50 m. E of Seringapatam. CANCAO, ATHIAN, HATIAN, OF KANG-KAO, & town and port of Annam, in the peninsula and 120 m. S of Cambodia, at the entrance of one of the deltoid arms of the May-kiang, named the Cancao, into the gulf of Siam, in N lat. 10° 15', E long. 105°. It is well fortified, and possesses an active commerce in betel-nuts, timber, iron, cotton, &c. The river is only navigable during the rainy season. C. was formerly the cap. of a small state—tributary to Cambodia—of the same name.
CANCELLARA, a town of Naples, in the prov. of the Basilicata, district and 9 m. NE of Potenza, in a beautiful valley. Pop. 3,110. It contains a fine old castle.
CANCELLI, a parish and village of Tuscany, in the prov. of Florence, 12 m. NW of Regello. Pop. 547. It contains numerous potteries.
CANCHAS, a town of Peru, in the prov. and 100 m. N of Lima, and 55 m. N of Chancay, on the r. bank of the Barranca.

CANCHE, a river of France, in the dep. of Pasde-Calais. It takes its rise in the cant. of Avesnes, near Magnicourt; passes Frevent, Vieil - Hesdin, Hesdin, Montreuil, and Etaples; and falls into the Atlantic, 5 m. below Etaples, after a course from E to W of 41 m., of which 9 are navigable.
CANCHE (LA), a village of France, in the dep. of the Côte-d'Or, cant. and 6 m. SE of Arnay-le- Duc. Pop. 397. It possesses some iron-works.

CANCON, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Lot-et-Garonne, arrond. of Villeneuve-sur-Lot. The cant. comprises 10 com., and in 1831 contained a pop. of 9,757. The town is 12 m. NNW of Villeneuve-sur-Lot. Pop. 1,641. It has 6 annual fairs.
CANCOOPA, or CANCOUPA, a town of Hindostan, cap. of a small district in Mysore, on a height 100 m. NE of Bedenore or Nuggur, and 140 m. NNW of Seringapatam.

CANCUN, an island of Mexico, near the NE extremity of the peninsula of Yucatan, at the mouth of the Bullina. It is 7 m. in length from N by E to S by W; but is very narrow. Its S end, which forms a very narrow channel with the mainland, is in N lat. 21° 2', W long. 86° 47' 9".
CANDA, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the district and 6 m. S of Badia, and 15 m. W of Bovigo. on the l. bank of the Castignaro canal. Pop. 1,500. It has one annual fair.
CANDADILLO POINT, a headland of Guatemala, forming the W side of the entrance of the gulf of Conchagua or Fonseca, in N lat. 13° 15', W long. 87° 46'.

CANDAHAR, or KANDAHAR', the principal city in Western Affghanistan, in N lat. 32° 20', E long. 66° 30' [Elphinstone]; N lat. 31° 37', E long. 65° 28' Walker's map]; 1,071 m. travelling distance from Delhi; and 2.074 m. from Calcutta. It is situated between the Turnuk and the Urghundab rivers, both branches of the Helmund; at an alt. above sea-level of 3,484 ft. It was the capital, in Ahmed Shah's time, of all his empire; but his son, Timur, removed the seat of government to Cabul. Its pop. according to Elphinstone's information, amounted to 100,000 souls; but it probably does not now exceed half that number. The form of the city is an oblong square, which is enclosed by a mud wall 27 ft. high, '26 ft. thick at the bottom, and 14th ft. at the top; and a newly-dug ditch about 9 ft. deep. The circumf. of the ramparts is nearly 4 m. Four long and broad bazaars meet in the centre of the town; and at their place of junction is a karsu or open square of 40 or 50 yards in breadth, covered with a dome, in which all the four streets meet, and which may be considered as the public market-place. Here proclamations are made, and the bodies of criminals exposed to the popular gaze. The four bazaars are each about 50 yards broad: the sides consisting of shops of the same size and plan, in front of which runs a uniform verandah for the whole length of the street. The shops are only one story high, and the lofty houses of the town are seen over them. There are gates issuing into the country at the end of all the bazaars, except the northern one, where stands the royal palace fronting the karsu. The external appearance of the palace is not remarkable; but it contains several courts and buildings, and a private garden. All the bazaars, except that leading to the palace, were once planted with trees, with a narrow canal running through the middle of each; but many of the trees have withered, and the canals are now no longer visible. The city, however, is well watered by two large canals drawn from the Urghundab, which flows about 4 m. to the W of the city. From these canals water-courses run to almost all parts of the town. All the other streets run from the four great bazaars; and, though narrow, they are all straight, and cross each other at right angles. The city is divided into several mohullahs or quarters, each of which belongs to one of the numerous tribes and nations forming the pop. of the place. Almost all the leading Durani families have houses in C., and some of them are said to be large and elegant. Mosques and caravanseras are also numerous. The tomb of Ahmed Shah, the founder of the city and dynasty, stands near the palace. It has a handsome cupola, and is elegantly painted, gilt, and ornamented within; but lit is constructed of coarse stone and bricks. It is highly venerated by all the Duranis. When any of the great lords are discontented, they commonly give out their intention of quitting the world, and spending their lives in prayer at this tomb. C. is chiefly built of mud and brick. The private houses are generally very poor dwellings. The Hindus, as usual, have the best houses of the common people, and adhere to their common custom of building them high. Ballad-singers and story-tellers are numerous in the bazaars; and all articles of merchandise from the West are in much greater plenty and perfection here than at Peshawur. The greater part of the pop. are Afghans; and the other inhabitants are Taujiks, Eimaks, Hindus, Persians, Seistans, Beluchis, and a few Usbecs, Arabs, Armenians, and Jews. The gardens and orchards round the town are numerous, and abound in delicious fruits; and there are many places of worship, where the inhabitants make parties more for pleasure than devotion. - When and by whom this city was founded is unknown. The oriental geographers attribute its foundation to Secunder Zulkernain, or Alexander the Great; and this notion has been adopted by several modern geographers and historians; amongst the latter, by Dr. Robertson, who makes it the Paropamisan Alexandria. But, as D'Anville remarks, the name Kandahar certainly does not come from Alexander, but from the Persian, or rather Turkish term kand, denoting 'a fortress.' Thus there is a place called Kandar in the Deccan, where no one will pretend that Alexander ever came; and there is another of the same name in Ajmeer; and Gandhara is the Sanscrit name for all the three. There is also a place called Caendar on the borders of the Attruck, NE of Meschid, which was a fortress of great importance in the times of Jenghis Khan and Tamerlane, corresponding to the Gandar or Gadar of Isidore of Charax, and the Gandarii of Herodotus and Pliny. A people called the Gandara are placed by Ptolemy between Suastene and the Indus,—a tract corresponding to the modern valley of Bunir. The ancient name of C., moreover, according to Kirkpatrick, quoted by Rennel, was Balicos; which completely sets aside the derivation of the name from Iscander, or Alexander. The Paropamisan Alexandria, it may also be noted, was built at the S foot of the Hindukhoosh, and at the N extremity of Paropamisus according to Arrian, whereas C. is to the S of the Paropamisus. The ancient city stood till the reign of Shah Hussein, who founded a new city under the name of Husseinabad. Nadir Shah again altered its site, and called it Nadirabad. Finally, Ahmed Shah founded the present city, and denominated it Ahmed Shauhee, and Ashreff-ool-Belaud, or 'the noblest of cities;' by which latter name and title it is mentioned in public documents, and in the language of the court during the Durani dynasty. C., with the surrounding country, was usually considered a province of the Persian empire. In the days of Akbar, both city and province were subject to the Mogul dynasty, and had been so from the time of Baber; but they were wrested from Jehan Ghir, the successor of Akbar, by Shah Abbas the Great. In 1638, C. was betrayed into the hands of Shah Jehan by Alinurdan Khan, the Persian governor, who was disgusted at the cruelty and incapacity of Shah Sefi. In 1650 it was recovered by Shah Abbas II., and remained in the Persian hands till the revolt of the Ghiljis, who possessed it under their hereditary chiefs till 1737, when the celebrated Nadir Shah, having expelled the Ghiljis, and reduced the Abdallies of Heraut, besieged it in the beginning of 1736, and took it by storm, after a siege of 18 months, put the garrison to the sword, totally demolished the fortifications, and removed the inhabitants to a new site, as above-mentioned. On the assassination of that great conqueror, in June, 1747, it fell into the hands of Ahmed-Shah, and during his lifetime, was the capital of the Afghan monarchy. On the dismemberment of Affghanistan, the brothers of Dost Mahommed established themselves at C.; but their rule was put an end to by the occupation of the city by British troops in 1839. These troops, 17,000 in number, maintained their position here under General Nott till August 1842, when C. was finally evacuated. The prov. of C., according to Abul Fazel, is bounded on the E by Sind; on the S by Sewi; on the W by Gurgistan; and on the N by Furreh and Cabul. To the NE of the cap. the aspect of C. is that of a desert. To the S the prospect is equally dreary; and W, towards the Helmund river, sterility continues to prevail. Inhabitants are thinly scattered over the district; and their lives are pastoral and migratory. Wheat, rice, grain, joari, dates, almonds, are amongst the productions. The domestic animals are camels and dogs; the latter possessing remarkable courage, activity, and strength. Amongst the wild animals are tigers, buffaloes, deer, and antelopes.—Elphinstone.—Conolly.—Allen.

CANDALLA, or CUNDALLA, a village of Hindostan, in the prov. of Aurungabad, on the verge of the Bhore Ghaut, near the falls of the Kallianie, on the road between, and nearly equidistant from, Poonah and Bombay. It contains a tolerable bazaar, and a government bungalow.
CANDAS, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Somme, cant. of Bernaville. Pop. 1,628.

CANDE', a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Maine-et-Loire, arrond. of Segré. The cant. comprises 6 com., and in 1831 contained a pop. of 8,779. The town is situated on the Erdre, 13 m. SW of Segré, and 25 m. WNW of Angers. Pop. 1,115. It possesses a considerable trade in wine, dried herbs, hemp, maize, oil, and prunes. Iron is wrought in the environs.

CANDEA, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Minas-Geraes, 20 m. W of Tamanduá. It derives its name from a species of climbing shrub which abounds in the locality, the branches of which are used for lights by the inhabitants.

CANDEAS. See JAMARI. CANDEISH, KHANDEISH, or KHANDESH, a province of Hindostan, in the Deccan, between the 20th and 22d parallels of N lat., and the meridians of 73° and 77°; bounded on the N by Malwa; on the E by Berar and Gundwana; on the S by Aurungabad and Berar; and on the W by Guzerat. It is 210 m. in length from E to W, with an average breadth of 80 m. Its surface is generally level; but its scenery is peculiarly rich and varied. It is interspersed with low barren hills, and nearly surrounded, except on the E where it joins the plain of Berar, by a mountain-cincture. The area includes much fertile land, being well-watered by streams, some of which are enclosed between extensive embankments. The Tupti and the Nerbudda are the principal rivers. The latter river forms the N boundary of C. In 1802, Jeswunt Row Holcar commenced the spoliation and ruin of this then fertile district; and Arabs, Bheels, and others carried on the most devastating warfare here until 1818, when these marauders fled before the British forces on the overthrow of the Mahratta power. The pop. may be classed under Bheels, Gonds, and Coolies. The principal towns are Burhampur, Asirghur, Hindia, Nundurbar, and Gaulna.—In the 15th cent. C. was an independent kingdom, governed by sovereigns who claimed descent from the caliph Omar.—The zillah or collecto- CAN 260 rate of C., consists of parts of the ancient prov. of C. acquired during the Mahratta war of 1818, and of villages and towns that belonged to Sindia, Holcar, the Nizam, and others. Its greatest length from E to W is 180 m.; greatest breadth 115 m. Area 12,527, or according to another admeasurement 12,430 sq. m. Pop. 480,000. In 1820, out of 2,700 villages in this district, no fewer than 1,100 had been altogether laid waste during the anarchy of the preceding thirty years.

CANDELA, a town of Naples, in the prov. of the Capitanate, 14 m. ESE of Bovina, and 6 m. SSW of Ascoli. Pop. 3,850. It contains 4 churches and an hospital. The environs produce excellent fruit, and are celebrated for their wine.
CANDELA, a town of Mexico, in the prov. and 33 m. WSW of Cohahuila.

CANDELARIA, a town of the island of Teneriffe, on the E coast, 15 m. SSE of Santa Cruz. It possesses a small port defended by a battery.—Also a town of La Plata, in the district and 180 m. E of Corrientes, on the l. bank of the Paranna. It possesses a fine parish church, from which it derives its name.—Also a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Pernambuco, district of Cabo-Santo-Agostinho, on the coast of the Atlantic, at the mouth of a river of the same name.—Also a town of Venezuela, in the prov. of Cumana, on an inosculation of the Guarapiche and Amana, 60 m. SSW of the entrance of the former river into the gulf of Paria.

CANDELARIA BAY, an indentation of the NE coast of the isthmus of Panama, on the W side of the bay of Choco, in N lat. 8° 10', formed by the low swampy ground at the mouths of the Atrato or Darien. Eight of these mouths are navigable for boats, but only one of them, the Little Faysan or Pheasant, presents good anchorage. CANDELARIA (BAXOS-DE-LA), OF CANDLEMAS
ISLANDS, a group of rocky islets in the Solomon archipelago, in S lat. 6° 20', and E long. 159° 20', discovered by Montana in 1567.
CANDELARIA (CAPE), a headland of Spain, in Galicia, 8 m. SW of Cape Ortegal, in N lat. 43° 44', and W long. 7° 56'.

CANDELARIAS, or CHANDELEURS, a string of islands in the gulf of Mexico, extending N by E 23 m., and forming with Cayo Breton, and Grand Grosier, a large gulf near the delta of the Mississippi. They are all low uninhabited islands, producing only a species of myrtle from which a green wax is extracted.

CANDELARO, a river of Naples, in the prov. of the Capitanate, which takes its rise in Monte Liburno, near S. Paolo, in the district of S. Severo, runs and after a total course of 45 m., flows into the gulf of Macedonia, 3 m. SSW of the town of that name. Its principal affluents are the Triolo, the Salsola, and the Celone, which unite with it on the r. Near its mouth it expands into the lagune of Pantano-Salso. Craven supposes this river to be the ancient Carbalus.

CANDELAY, or GANTALAWE, a beautiful artificial lake in the island of Ceylon, situated in an extensive and broad valley, 28 m. SW from Trincomalee. In the centre of the valley, around which the ground gradually ascends towards the distant hills that envelope it, a causeway, extending upwards of 1 m., made of masses of rock, has been constructed to retain the waters that from every side pour into the space enclosed within the circumjacent hills and the artificial dam thus formed. During the rainy season, when the lake attains its greatest elevation, the area of ground, over which the inundation extends, may be computed at 15 sq. m.; and such is the capricious nature of the mountain-streams in this tropical island, where beavy rain frequently falls without intermission for many successive days, that no common barrier would suffice to resist the great, sudden, and enormous pressure that must be sustained on such occasions. Aware of the peculiarity in the character of their rivers, the ancient Cingalese built the retaining wall that supports the waters of the lake of C. with such solidity. and massiveness, as to defy the utmost fury of the mountain-torrents. Nearly the whole of its extent is formed with hewn masses of rock, from 8 to 10 ft. in length, to move which, by sheer physical force, must have been a work of no small difficulty.-De Butts.—Forbes.
CANDELEDA, a town of Spain, in New Castile, prov. and 78 m. W of Toledo, and 14 m. NNE of Oropesa, on the l. bank of a river of the same name.
CANDELO, a town of Piedmont, cap. of a mandimento, in the prov. and 4 m. SE of Biella, on the r. bank of the Cervo.
CANDELORO, a small town of Asiatic Turkey, in the pash. of Itchil, sanjak and 33 m. WNW of Alaya, on the gulf of Adalia.

CANDES, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Indre-et-Loire, cant. and 9 m. WNW of Chinon, on the l. bank of the Loire, at the confluence of the Vienne. Pop. 748. It possesses extensive cooperages.

CA'NDIA, or CRETE, the ancient Creta, one of the largest islands in the Mediterranean, forming the Ejalet Kirid, or 'Country of Crete,' of the Turkish empire. It is called by the Turks Kirid, and commonly by Europeans Candia, -which is perhaps a corruption of Khandak, a name given by the Spanish Moors or Arabians to a town which they built upon it soon after their conquest of the island in the early part of the 9th cent.; but it is supposed by some to allude to the white appearance of its mountains as seen from the sea, when snow-clad. Situation and extent. This island is situated between 34° 52' and 35° 40' N lat., and 23° 30' and 26° 25' E long. Its shores are washed on the N by that part of the Archipelago which is sometimes called the sea of C.; on the NE are the straits of Scarpanto, which separate the island of that name from C.; the Mediterranean bathes its southern shores; and on the NW are the straits of Cerigotto. Its NW extremity is about 23 leagues SSE from Cape Matapan in the Morea; and its NE point, 37 leagues SSW from Anatolia; so that this island forms a kind of intermediate ground betwixt Europe and Asia; and may serve to fix the dubious limits between the Archipelago and the Mediterranean. It is about 172 m. in length; its average breadth is 25 m., but towards the middle it has a breadth of 35 m. Its superficial extent is estimated by Hassel at 4,325 sq. m.; by Stein at 4,234 sq. m.; by M. C. Moreau at 5,200 sq. m. Physical features.] It cannot escape notice that while the greater part of the islands of the Archipelago have a direction, in their extreme length, from N to S, with a greater or less inclination to the E or W, the position of C. is exactly the reverse. Its coasts are very irregular and deeply indented. The N coast affords some excellent harbours and secure roadsteds; the S is elevated and nearly unapproachable. The gulf of Kisamos, on the coast of which are the ruins of ancient Aptera, is formed by the two promontories of Buso and Spada. The gulf of Canea presents an excellent roadsted to the navigator; that of Suda is considered to be the best shelter for vessels in the whole island. Cape Matala forms the most southern point of Europe. - The island is traversed throughout its whole length by a chain of calcareous mountains, between which lie the valleys or plains of Gortyna, Candia, Canea, and Girapetro. This long chain presents three principal points. On the W are the Sphakiot mountains, or the Sphakiottici or Asprovouna, the Leuca Ore of the ancients, so called from their retaining the snow on their summits throughout eight or nine months of the year. In the centre is Mount Psilorii, the Ida of the ancients, which rises to the height of 1,200 toises or 7,690 ft. above sea-level; and of which the summit is covered with snow throughout a great part of the year. The circumference of its base is not less than 25 leagues. It consists of a group of hills heaped one above another in a pyramidical form. The E part of the central chain is composed of Mounts Lasiti, Cavontci, and Sethia, the ancient Dictaea, which gently slope toward the coast. The greater part of these mountains are deficient in wood; but in some quarters they are clothed with forests of cypresses, plantains, and carobs; extensive districts are covered with aromatic herbs and shrubs. "The whole atmosphere of the island," says a recent visitor, "has an aromatic odour arising from the flowers and shrubs which grow wild among the rocks, and furnish food for the hares and wild goats." The torrents which descend from the mountains, and which are the only rivers on the island, usually disappear in summer; but there are numerous springs which supply the purposes of irrigation. The calcareous rock which forms the basis of the island is easily formed into caverns; and no country in the world, perhaps, abounds more in natural excavations. Granite, schist, and slate are amongst the primary rocks. Climate, soil, and productions.] The climate of C. is mild and salubrious on the coast and higher grounds. The extremes of heat and cold are here equally unknown; for, although situated at nearly equal distances betwixt the coasts of Africa and Europe, the heat is tempered in summer by the wind which the natives call enbat, which blows in the Archipelago from the N throughout the greater part of the day. The mean temp. is 64°. Sometimes, however, the therm. reaches 88° in summer; and the inland valleys which have rivulets running through them, are, from June to the end of September, very unhealthy. Little rain falls during the summer; but heavy dews preserve the freshness and brilliance of the vegetation; and in autumn plentiful showers fall. Snow falls on the mountains from Nov. to Feb. The severest storms are from the NW. Earthquakes are frequently felt in the N parts of the island. The leprosy is the only endemic disorder known.-The greater portion of the soil is dry and stony, and illadapted for the culture of the cerealia; although, were the inhabitants at all industrious, sufficient grain might be raised for the home-consumption. Lint, cotton, and tobacco are cultivated; but fruits, wine, and oil form the staple-productions of the country. The best wine is made at Arcadi on Mount Ida, and at Malevisi near Candia: the former from a white grape, the latter from a red. Old Malevisi or Malmsey is greatly esteemed. The common wine is light and refreshing. Ladanum gum, gathered from the leaves and stems of the cistus, is another staple production. About 50 quintals of this drug = 5,200 lbs. are annually exported to Constantinople, where it is used as a perfume. Whole fields are sown with lupins, which is a very common food with the Candians; but many useful plants, for which no soil or climate is more propitious than that of C., are almost completely neglected. The mulberry-tree, which thrives wonderfully in this island, is scarce. Flax is tolerably plentiful, but not sufficient for the wants of the inhabitants; and though cotton thrives well, they draw their chief supplies from Smyrna, and the environs of Ephesus. Sesamum occupies some spots in the plains; but instead of extracting oil from its seed, the inhabitants use ! it only for mixing with their bread to give it flavour. The olive thrives everywhere, and attains an extreme age and immense size here, although the only culture it receives is the ploughing of the surrounding soil. The Malevisi raisins are highly esteemed; they are principally prepared at Milopotamo. Myrtles and rose-laurels overshadow all the rivulets; and the plains and pastures are enamelled with flowers of every hue and fragrance. Tournefort and Olivier have explored the botany of this island. - About 600,000 sheep and goats are reared on the mountains. The goats of C. are a remarkably fine breed; and the sheep are highly esteemed for the delicacy of their mutton. Of horned cattle, there may be 50,000. The C. mules are much esteemed in the East. The plains are well-stocked with game, particularly red partridges and golden plovers; and the groves and gardens are filled with a variety of singing-birds, among which are the linnet, the nightingale, the petty-chap, the gold-finch, the bull-finch, the lark, and the thrush. This last mentioned bird is taken in immense quantities by the peasants, in the winter season, and carried to market. But the most melodious and valuable is the Tardus cyanus, or Solitary black-bird, which inhabits the summits of the mountains, and fills the desert with its music. It is called by the Greeks petro-cockiso, or 'bird of the rocks,' and by the Turks kajabulbul, or 'nightingale of the rock.' It is in great request throughout Turkey for its charming melody, and is seldom bought for less than 100 dollars.-The reptiles common to the other islands of the Archipelago are found here. The lizards are accounted venomous by the inhabitants, but Olivier affirms that they are quite harmless. Pliny, indeed, mentions the tarantula as an inhabitant of C.; and Belon enumerates three species of serpents which were known here,the ophis, the ochendra, and the ephloti. The tarantula occurs in C., and another species of spider equally dangerous with the tarantula, which is of the same size, but lives constantly in subterraneous retreats. Commerce.] Brandy, oil, fruits, honey, soap, and cheese are the principal articles of exportation. The oil of Mirabello and Stia is almost all employed in the manufacture of soap, which is sent principally to Constantinople, its exportation into Christendom being rigidly prohibited. The average crop of the olive-trees is calculated to produce 2,000,000 gallons of oil, although one-fourth is generally lost for want of hands to gather it. The fruit undergoes three successive pressures in the mill; the oil produced by the first is of course the best. Silk is raised in small quantities, but of good quality. About 2,000 tons of carobs are produced; and oranges and lemons form a considerable article of export. Mr. Bowring reports that the soap manufactories of C. could produce 6,000 tons of soap annually, at an average cost, exclusive of oil, of 7s. 9d. per cwt. The quality is excellent. The ports of Retimo, Canea, and Candia, before the breaking out of the civil war, exported oil and soap to the value of £100,000 annually, although the trade was much shackled by the absurd regulations of the Turks. The French papers, a few years ago, noticed the discovery of two coal-mines in C., one upon the N coast, about 4 hours' journey from Retimo; the other upon the S coast at Preveli, near Sphakia, a short distance from the sea. In May 1839, it is said, 50 quintals of coal were raised from the first-named mine, and sent to Egypt; and a new survey having been demanded, 1,363 quintals were raised in six days, and forwarded to Retimo. The cost of carriage was estimated at 2 francs 75 cents, the 100 the two mines, was essayed in one of the steamers belonging to the French squadron in the Levant. The sample from the mine at Retimo was chiefly in pyrite; that from Preveli was in a ligneous state. It was thought that if the mines were regularly worked, they would supply good produce, and add to the revenue of the island. By a report for 1842 on the state of this island, it appears that since its withdrawal from the government of Mehemet Ali, the cleaning out of the ports has been abandoned; and that those of C. and Retimo are fast filling up. Alkali, . . . . . . . 1,200,000 Total, . . 11,049,000 Population.] The Achaians, the Eteocretes, the Cydonians, the Dorian Trichians, and the Pelasgians were among the early inhabitants of this island, which was in ancient times extremely populous. The poets describe it as containing 100 cities. If, with Savary, we reckon the average pop. of these cities to have been 6,000 souls, their total pop. would be 600,000; and, supposing the other inhabitants of the island to have been equal in number, the whole amount would be 1,200,000 souls. The present pop. has been variously estimated. Savary, in 1779, estimated it at 350,200; of whom 200,000 were Turks, 150,000 Greeks, and 200 Jews. On the breaking out of the Greek revolution in 1821, there were only 260,000 inhabitants remaining in Crete, the result of 150 years' domination of the Turks; and at the close of the revolution, the inhabitants had dwindled down to 150,000, of whom 100,000 were Greek rayahs, 26,000 Turks, and 24,000 foreigners, chiefly Greeks from the Morea, or from the Ionian isles. There were also about 2,000 black male and female slaves on the island. The cause of this decrease since 1821 is to be found in the war carried on by the Cretans against both the Sultan and Mehemet Ali, and in the oppressive character of the Egyptian domination. Mr. Pashley estimates the pop. in 1834 at only 129,000. kilog. In February 1840, this coal, the produce of I - The C., both Mahommedans and Christians, are in general tall and vigorous men, and particularly skilful in the use of the bow,—a weapon which frequently appears on ancient Cretan medals. Among the Mahommedan part of the pop. we must rank the Abadiotes, the descendants of those Saracens whom Nicephorus Phocas expelled from C. in the 10th cent. They are a smaller and weaker race than the other inhabitants, speaking the Arabian language, and located in the neighbourhood of Ida.—The Candiotes are extremely ignorant, superstitious, and bigoted. In 1817 there were only three schools, attended by 80 scholars, in the whole island. The Greek dialect spoken in C. is very corrupt. Turkish is spoken in the greatest purity at Candia. The rural pop. understand only Greek; but the aghas, in the principal towns and villages, also know Turkish, although Greek is their mother-tongue. "As to the peasant, when he has said 'salám aleikúm,' or replied 'aleikúm salám,' he has exhausted the whole stock of his Mahommedan lore." [Pashley.] But the Greek of the C. peasant has many peculiarities of its own. The kalogheros or monk of C. is scarcely raised a single step above the peasant. Few of them can read. They are, in fact, cuiefly distinguished from the class of labourers by their long beards, their vow of celibacy, and by living in a monastery. The pateras can all read, and write a little; but few can spell. The native Candians are of the Greek church, and are allowed the free exercise of their religion; but are prohibited from repairing their religious houses or places of worship; by costly bribes, however, they obtain permission from the pashas, so far as to prevent them falling entirely to ruin. The island previous to 1830 was divided into 12 bishoprics, viz. Gortynia, Cnossou, Mirabella, Hyera, Girapetro, Arcadia, Cherronesse, Lambis, Milopotamo, Retimo, Canea, and Cisamo. These have been reduced to 8. The bishop of Gortynia is appointed by the patriarch of Constantinople, and assumes the title of archbishop. He wears a triple tiara, writes his signature in red ink, answers for all the debts of the clergy, and nominates to all vacant bishoprics in the island. He resides at Candia; and is the only Greek who enjoys the privilege of entering that city on horseback. Government.] The island of C. is divided into the three sanjaks of Candia, Caneo, and Retimo, so called from their respective capitals; and these are subdivided into eparchies. The sub-government is intrusted to a pasha of three tails, who resides at Candia, and under whom are two other pashas. The viceroy of Egypt assured Mr. Bowring that this island had been, in a pecuniary point of view, always onerous to him. Its revenue did not exceed, on an average, 400,000 dollars, or £80,000, of which onefourth was paid to the governor. The following is an approximate table of the revenues of the island: Piastres.
