Towns.] Candia, the capital, according to Tournefort, is built on the site of the ancient Cytoum or Matium. It is inhabited by about 12,000 Turks, 3,000 Greeks, and 50 Jews. It offers a splendid coup-d'æil from the sea; but on the land-side its high walls and advanced works conceal the view from the spectator. The houses are well-built; but seldom rise to two stories, and present no exterior facade, so that the streets appear to run between two parallel walls, the monotonous uniformity of which is only broken by gates here and there, and occasionally a patch of trellis-work covered with vines. The harbour is a mere basin formed by two moles which project about 250 yards into the sea; but it might contain 40 vessels if it were cleared of the sand which at present chokes it, and renders it inaccessible to vessels exceeding 100 tons. Large vessels anchor under the island of Standia, abreast of and 3 m. distant from the harbour. In the neighbourhood of the town is the Turkish cemetery, a favourite promenade of the Turks.-The sanjak of Candia forms the E part of the island, extending from Mount Psilorii to Cape Salemone. — Canea is a fortified town on the NW coast, with 7,150 inhabitants. Its port is the most frequented. The sanjak of Canea forms the W part of C .- Retimo is situated on the ruins of the ancient Rhitymnæ. It is a pleasant little town, with about 5,000 inhabitants. Its port is very small.—At the E end of the island is the port of Spinalonga; and a few miles to the S of it is the port of Ayio Nicola. But the port of Suda, 3 m. from Canea, is the best in the island. The Labyrinth.] Crete, which anciently contained so many cities, and was the centre of arts, commerce, and politeness, must still possess many remains of antiquity. These have, in many cases, been minutely described by different travellers; but to particularize their various descriptions would be a task by far too extensive for us. One of its monuments, however, has so much attracted the attention of the curious, and is in itself so astonishing, that it demands a more particular description. This monument is an intricate mazy cavern, called the Labyrinth, in the neighbourhood of the ancient Gortynia, 20 m. inland from the town of Candia,—the residence of the famed but fabulous Minotaur. The mouth of the labyrinth is natural, and very narrow; but, advancing a little way, we find a wide passage with a flat roof cut in the rock above. Out of this you ascend by a narrow path which strikes off from the right side of the entrance; and a person is here obliged to creep on his hands and feet for 100 paces, because the roof is very low. At the end of this narrow passage, the ceiling suddenly rises to a considerable elevation, and various roads open on each side and cross each other in different directions. They are generally about 7 ft. high, and from 6 to 10 ft., or sometimes more, in wideness; and they are all cut with a chisel in the rock. It is impossible to enumerate or describe the variety or complication of the winding paths which are cut in the rock, Some of them form curves leading imperceptibly to a wide space, the roof of which is supported by huge pillars, and where three or four roads meet running in opposite directions; others, extending in a curved direction for a considerable way, end in several ramifications which are carried to a great length, and terminated by the rock. "We travelled cautiously," says Savary, "through the windings of this vast labyrinth, amidst eternal shades, the obscurity of which our torches could scarcely penetrate. In such a place as this, imagination calls up a numerous train of frightful or fantastic images -she fancies steep precipices ready to ensnare the feet of the curious observer,-monsters ready to spring upon him from every corner,-in a word, a thousand chimeras which have no real existence." "After walking," the same traveller adds, "for a considerable time through the dreadful cavern of the Minotaur, we arrived at the extremity of the passage whose direction To the right there is another nearly of the same form. Each of them may be about 24 or 30 ft. square. By the time we got persuaded that it would be impossible for a person to find his way out if he were left alone in this cavern without either thread or torch. The horrors of the place, and the thick darkness, would fill his soul with terror, and he would perish miserably." Several conjectures have been formed by different writers concerning the origin of this cavern. That it was formed in part at least by Nature, is evident from the rudeness of many of the different passages. The art of man, it is probable, did little more than finish what Nature had commenced,—widening passages where they were narrow, and connecting such as were already formed by other windings for the purpose of increasing the intricacy. Cockerell has given an account and plan of this singular cavern or excavation in Walpole's Collection of Travels in the East, vol. ii. History.] C. has borne different appellations in ancient as well as modern times. It was called Æria, Idaa from Mount Ida, Curetia from the Curetes, and 'The Fortunate island,' from the serenity of its climate and the richness of its productions. It enjoyed the paternal government of Minos, who was succeeded by a line of royal chiefs; but a republican government was established upon this island previous to its subjugation by the Romans. From these conquerors C. passed to the emperors of the East, who retained it until 823. The Saracens established themselves in C. in the 9th cent.; but were expelled in 952. The Genoese having got possession of this island ceded it to Boniface, marquess of Montserrat, who sold it in 1204 to the Venetians, who retained possession of it till 1669, when, after a disastrous war of 24 years, in the last three of which 29,000 Christians and 70,000 Turks were killed, it fell into the hands of the Turks, who have possessed it ever since. "Before the outbreaking of the Greek revolution," says Mr. Pashley, "C. was the worst-governed prov, of the Turkish empire; the local authorities were wholly unable to control the license of the janissaries, who consisted solely of Cretan Mahommedans, and made it a point of honour not to suffer any one of their number to be brought to justice for any ordinary crime." The Sfakians alone preserved their independence, and became the nucleus round which the Christian Cretans organized their resistance to their Mahommedan oppressors in 1821. From 1822 to 1830, C. was an object of special attention to the viceroy of Egypt; and the three allied powers decided that it should remain under his government. The oppressive sway of Mehemet Ali was fast reducing the inhabitants of this fertile and beautiful island to the condition of the wretched fellah population of Egypt, when the allied powers again interposed, and withdrew it from his government, and placed it once more under the dominion of the Porte.

CANDIA, a township of Rockingham co., in the state of New Hampshire, U.S., 18 m. SE of Concord. It is generally hilly, and reaches in the NW to the White Hills, or Mount Candia. It is well cultivated, and possesses considerable fertility. Pop. 1,430.
CANDIA, a town of Piedmont, 10 m. S of Ivrea, and 9 m. N of Chivasso.
CANDIA - DI - LOMELLINA, a town of Piedmont, 10 m. WSW of Montara, on an affluent of the Sesia. Pop. 1,400.
CANDIANA, a village of Venetian Lombardy, in the gov. of Venice, delegation and 15 m. SSE of Padua, and 6 m. E of Conselve. Pop. 2,000.
CANDILARIA, a small lake of Mexico, in the prov. and 180 m. NNW of Chihuahua.
CANDIOTE, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of São-Pedro-do-Rio-Grande, on the Serra das Tapes, between Santa-Tecla and Canguçú.
CANDLEMAS ISLANDS, a group of islands in the South Atlantic ocean, to the N of the Sandwich islands, in S lat. 57° 10′, W long. 29° 40′.
CANDLESBY, a parish of Lincolnshire, 31 m. NE of Spilsby. Area 850 acres. Pop. in 1841, 247.

CANDONGA, a mountain of Brazil, in the prov. of Minas-Geraes and district of Serro. It affords pasturage to numerous herds of cattle, and produces in considerable quantities a species of plant locally called capitâo-do-mato, which is used as a substitute for the tea-plant.

CANDOR, a township of Tioga co., in the state of New York, U. S., 8 m. N of Owego. It possesses a hilly surface, and is drained by Cottolong and West Owega creeks. The soil, consisting of loam, is generally fertile. Pop. in 1840, 3,370.—Also a village of Washington co., in the state of Pennsylvania, 227 m. W of Harrisburg. Pop. 223.
CANDOVER-BROWN, a parish of the co. of | Huelva, and 3 m. S of Ayamonti. Southampton, 5 m. NNW of Arlesford. Area 1,230 acres. Pop. in 1841, 313.
CANDOVER-CHILTON, a parish of the co. of CAN Southampton, 5 m. N of New Arlesford. Area 2,190 acres. Pop. in 1841, 103.
CANDOVER-PRESTON, a parish of the co. of Southampton, 6 m. NNE of New Arlesford. Area 3,430 acres. Pop. in 1841, 481.
CANDOZA, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Beira, comarca and 20 m. SSE of Viseu.
CANDY. See CEYLON and KANDY.

CANDY (CAPE), a promontory of the island of Celebes, on the N coast, in N lat. 1° 10', E long 121° 35'. CANE, KEANE, or KEN RIVER, a river of Hindostan, in Bundlecund, which takes its rise in the Vindhya mountains, near Mohar, to the NE of Belhari; runs N; and after a course of 230 m. unites with the Jumna, at Chilatara, 39 m. below the confluence of the Betwa, and 18 m. SW of Futtehpur. Its principal affluents on the l. are the Sonar and the Oormel. Its channel is extremely rocky, and contains fine agates and jasper, and its waters abound with fish. This river is supposed to be the Camas of Pliny.

CANEA, or KHANIA' [ITAL. La Canea], a town of the island of Candia, cap. of the sanjak of the same name, on the N coast, 75 m. W of Candia, in N lat. 35° 28′ 40″, E long. 24°. Pop. about 6,000, of whom 5,000 are Mahommedans; the rest are Christians and Jews. It is surrounded by a strong wall flanked with bastions, and a deep ditch; and is defended by a citadel. It has only one gate. The houses are well-built, and the streets broad and well-paved. There are six mosques and several churches, and a capuchin convent. The port-although the best in the island-is small, and possesses a narrow entrance, with about 24 ft. water; but has an excellent building-dock and arsenals. It faces to the N; has a mole 1,160 French ft. in length; and is protected by a fort, on which is a light-house. Extensive manufactories of soap exist here; and this town is the principal commercial entrepot in the island. Its export-trade consists chiefly in oil, soap, silk, fruit, and wool. C. is supposed to occupy the site of the ancient Cydonia.—The Venetians founded the modern city in 1252. In 1645, C. was taken by the Turks from the Venetians after a siege of 50 days.— The sanjak of C. comprises the entire eastern portion of the island, extending 44 m, in length from E to W, and about 30 m. in average breadth. It is generally mountainous; but produces grain, oil, fruit, lint, cotton, silk, and honey.
CANE, or Canis Island, a small island near the N coast of Tunis, 10 m. NNE of Cape Zibib, in N lat. 37° 20′ 15″, E long. 10° 5′.

CANEADEA, a township of Alleghany co., in the state of New York, 10 m. N of Angelica. It is intersected by the Genessee and its tributaries. The soil consists of clay and sandy loam. Pop. 1,633. The village lies on the E side of the Genessee, and consists of 12 or 15 dwellings.
CANEGGIO, a parish and village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Tessin, district and 3 m. NE of Mendrisio, and 8 m. SE of Lugano, near the l. bank of the Muggio. Pop. 379.
CANEGHEM, a commune of Belgium, in West Flanders, 19 m. NE of Courtrai. Pop. 1,964. It possesses extensive manufactories of linen.
CANEL, a town of Senegambia, in the kingdom of Futatoro, 40 m. SW of Sedo, in a fertile district near the l. bank of the Guilulu. Pop. 6,000. Iron is wrought in the vicinity.
CANELA, a small island near the S coast of Spain, at the mouth of the Guadiana, in the prov. of

CANELAS, or Caneles, a mining town of Mexico, in the prov. and 110 m. NW of Durango, and 15 m. S of Topio, on the W slope of the Sierra Madre, on the upper part of the Tamazula river, a headstream of the Culiacan. The streets are necessarily narrow. To the inhabitants this is no inconvenience, for a wheeled vehicle of any kind has never been seen in C.; indeed, it would be impossible for one to ascend the mountain at any point. The climate of C. is perpetual spring and summer. The trees are perpetually green; for, as fast as the leaves fade and fall, others are fast expanding; added to cile. which, the golden harvests of the orange-tree are ever beautiful to the eye. The mountains of C. have ever been celebrated, from the earliest discovery of the country, for their richness in silver; but from the poverty of the ore, the mines have never been extensively worked. Veins of quicksilver have been found; but these have never been worked successfully. The workers in the mines of C. are most grievously affected with the loathsome disease bronchocele, or goitre.
CANELLAS, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Tras-os-Montes, comarca and 10 m. SSE of Villa- Real, near the r. bank of the Douro.

CANELLI, a town of Piedmont, cap. of a mandemento, in the prov. and 20 m. SE of Asti, near the l. bank of the Belbo. Pop. 3,500. It contains 2 churches and a convent, and has 12 annual fairs. Limestone is quarried in the environs.
CANELON, or GUADALUPE, a town of the Banda Oriental del Uruguay, on a river of the same name, 20 m. N of Monte Video.
CANELOS (Los), aborigines of New Granada, who inhabit the district between the Napo and Curaray. They derive their name from an indigenous species of cinnamon with which the district abounds.
CANELOS (SAN-JOSE-DE), a small town of New Granada, in the prov. and 110 m. SE of Quito, and 60 m. NNE of Macas.
CANEMORTO, a town of the States-of-the- Church, in the delegation and 20 m. SSE of Rieti, and 30 m. NE of Rome.
CANERO (SAN MIGUEL DE), a village of Spain, in Asturias, in the prov. and 30 m. from Oviedo, in a mountainous locality N of Luarca. Pop. 1,660.
CANE SPRING, a village of Bullit co., in the state of Kentucky, U. S., 62 m. SW of Frankfort. Pop. 923.
CANESUS, a lake in the state of New York, U. S., in Livington co. It is 8 m. in length, and 3 m. in breadth, and discharges itself into the Genessee.
CANET, a commune of France, in the dep. of Hérault, cant. of Clermont. Pop. 1,003.
CANET (CAPE), a headland of Spain, in the prov. and 20 m. NE of Valencia, in N lat. 39° 40'.
CANET (LE), a village of France, in the dep. of Var, cant. of Lorgues, on a height 12 m. SSW of Draguignan. Pop. 933. It contains a crystal manufactory, and in the environs is a mine of lead.
CANET-DE-MAR, a town of Spain, in Catalonia, in the prov. and 29 m. NE of Barcelona, on the Mediterranean. It possesses manufactories of lace, and distilleries of brandy.
CANETE, a town of Spain, in New Castile, cap. of a judicial partido, in the prov. and 32 m. ENE of Cuenca, on an affluent of the Cabriel.

CANETE, a town and port of Peru, cap. of the prov. of the same name, in the intendancy and 80 m. SSE of Lima, on the Pacific, near the mouth of the river of the same name. The port is small and unsheltered. The prov. of C. extends about 90 m. along the shore of the Pacific, and produces wheat, maize, sugar, fruit of all kinds, salt, and saltpetre.
CANETE-DE-LAS-TORRES, a town of Spain, in Andalusia, prov. and 25 m. E of Cordova, and 4 m. E of Bujalance, near the r. bank of the Canalejo.
CANETE-LA-REAL, a town of Spain, in Anda- I CAN lusia, prov. and 32 m. WNW of Malaga, and 14 m. NE of Ronda.
CANET-LO-ROIG, a town of Spain, in Valencia, prov. of Castellon-de-la-Plana, 20 m. NNW of Pensacola. Pop. 2,000. It contains distilleries of brandy. Silk and honey are cultivated in the environs.
CANEVA, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the delegation of Udine, district and 3 m. NW of Sa-
CANEWDON, a parish of Essex, 3 m. NNE of Rochford. Area 2,800 acres. Pop. in 1841, 723.
CANEY, a tributary of the Rio Brazos, in Texas, flowing into that river on the r. bank, 20 m. S of Washington.

CANEY FORK, a river of the state of Tennessee, U. S., which takes its rise in the Cumberland mountains, in the SE of Warren co.; runs NW along the confines of White co.; enters Smith co.; and unites with Cumberland river opposite Carthage, after a total course of about 75 m.
CANEY (OLD), a stream of Texas, in Brazoria co., about 45 m. in length, which flows into the gulf of Mexico about 18 m. W of the San Bernard.
CANFIELD (GREAT), a parish of Essex, 32 m. SW of Great Dunmow, on the Roding. Area 3,070 acres. Pop. in 1841, 496.
CANFIELD (LITTLE), a parish of Essex, 3 m. WSW of Great Dunmow. Area 1,510 acres. Pop. in 1841, 130.
CANFIELD, a township of Trumbull co., in the state of Ohio, U. S., 164 m. ENE of Columbus, and 14 m. SE of Warren. Its soil is generally productive. Pop. 1,279.
CANFOO, a town of Soudan, in the territory of Houssa, and kingdom of Zegzeg, on an affluent of the Geon, 40 m. NNE of Eggebee, and about the same distance S of Kano.

CANFORD MAGNA, a parish of Dorset, 2 m. E of Wimborne-Minster, on the Stour. It includes the chapelries of Kingstone and Parkstone, and the tything of Longfleet. Area 16,870 acres. Pop. in 1841, 3,957.

CANFRANC, a town of Spain, in Aragon, in the prov. and 45 m. NNW of Huesca, and 12 m. N of Jaca, in a valley, and on a river an affluent of the Aragon, of the same name, and in the vicinity of one of the most frequented routes across the Pyrenees.
CANGA, a town of Congo, on the r. bank of the Coango, opposite Condi, or Pembo de Ocango, and 20 m. SE of the confluence of that river with the Congo or Zaire.
CANGALLO, a town of Peru, cap. of the district of the same name, in the prov. of Ayacucho, on an affluent of the Mantaro, 40 m. S of Huamanga.
CANGALLOS, a town of Peru, in the prov. of Ayacucho, and 25 m. S of Huamanga, on an affluent of the Mantaro.
CANGAS (SANTA EULA'LIA DE), a town of Spain, in Galicia, in the prov. and 15 m. SW of Pontevedra, at the base of a mountain on the N side of the bay of Vigo. Pop. 2,470.

CANGAS DE ONIS (SANTA MARIA DE), a town of Spain, in Asturias, in the prov. and 35 m. ESE of Oviedo, in a fertile and salubrious locality, on the r. bank of the Vua, at the confluence of the Chico. Pop. 4,115. It is the Concana of antiquity. In the environs are the celebrated abbey of Cobadonga, and the monastery of Sao Pedro de Villanosa.
CANGAS DE TINEO, a town of Spain, in Asturias, in the prov. and 35 m. WSW of Oviedo, in a level tract, watered by the Narcea. Pop. 502.
